unitTitle: KS2 Y3 Stop-frame animation (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Can a picture move? — flip books
lessons.objectives:
I can say that an animation is a sequence of pictures
I can put pictures in the right order to show movement
I can make a flip book and say how it works
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: animation · sequence · frame · flip book. TA: each pupil has a small sticky-note book and the starter worksheet. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Recap: today we start a new unit about making pictures move.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is an animation? Show the flip book picture. Explain: an animation is lots of still pictures shown fast, one after another, so it looks like it moves. On the starter worksheet pupils ORDER three pictures of a wave so the arm goes up.
   • Support: which picture comes FIRST (arm down).  • Core: order all three.  • Challenge: say why the order matters.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — how to draw a flip book. Teacher models: draw near the open edge, start on the bottom sheet, draw the part that moves first. Likely error: draws the new picture in a different place each time → fix-words “only move it a little bit.”
3. (20–40) You-do — make a stick person wave. Pupils draw a small change on each page, then flip the book. On the activity worksheet they answer where to draw (single-choice) and tick the true facts about flip books (multi-select). TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: choose where to draw the stick person.  • Core: choose which sheet to start on.  • Challenge: explain why small changes work best.
4. (40–50) Plenary — show a flip book; self-tick the 3 “I can…”. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
Differentiation on the SAME task — every flip book looks finished.

lessons.title: Frame by frame — using the animation app
lessons.objectives:
I can say that each photo is called a frame
I can find the buttons in the animation app
I can take frames to make a stop-frame animation
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: stop-frame animation · frame · photo · capture · onion skinning. TA: tablets set on stands, the animation app open, starter worksheet out. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Recap: last lesson our flip books made pictures move.
1. (0–10) Starter — from paper to screen. Show the app home screen. Explain: instead of drawing every page, we take photos. Each photo is a FRAME. On the starter worksheet pupils ORDER the steps to start a new movie.
   • Support: which button starts a new movie.  • Core: order the start-up steps.  • Challenge: say why the tablet must stay still.
2. (10–22) I-do → we-do — meet the app. Teacher shows the capture screen: take a photo, move the drawing a little, take another. Onion skinning shows the last frame faintly so you know where to draw next. Likely error: moves the tablet between photos → fix-words “keep the tablet still.”
3. (22–42) You-do — make a stick person move. Pupils take 6–10 frames of a small movement. On the activity worksheet they LABEL the app screen (capture button, stop button, onion skin), then choose what onion skinning shows (single-choice). TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: find the capture button.  • Core: label all three parts.  • Challenge: say why onion skinning helps.
4. (42–50) Plenary — play one animation back; self-tick the 3 “I can…”. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
Note: the source ‘making your animation’ clip is teacher-played only.

lessons.title: What's the story? — planning with a storyboard
lessons.objectives:
I can name the setting, the characters and the events in a story
I can put the parts of a story in order on a storyboard
I can choose a story that is simple enough to animate
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: setting · character · event · storyboard. TA: starter worksheet and the printed scene picture ready; keep all sheets for Lesson 4. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Recap: last lesson we made the app take frames.
1. (0–10) Starter — three story words. Show the scene picture. Teach: the SETTING is where it happens, a CHARACTER is who is in it, an EVENT is what happens. On the starter worksheet pupils SORT words into setting / character / event.
   • Support: which word is the setting.  • Core: sort all the words.  • Challenge: add one event of your own.
2. (10–22) I-do → we-do — keep it simple. Teacher shows that card characters can’t turn round or sit down, and each new background is extra work. So choose one setting and two characters. Likely error: plans too much to animate → fix-words “one place, two characters, one small event.”
3. (22–42) You-do — make a storyboard. Pupils draw three boxes: start, middle, end. On the activity worksheet they ORDER three storyboard pictures, then choose which plan is easiest to animate (single-choice). TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: order start–middle–end.  • Core: pick the easy plan.  • Challenge: say why the busy plan is hard.
4. (42–50) Plenary — share a storyboard; self-tick the 3 “I can…”. Keep all sheets. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: A scene with a setting and characters (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Picture perfect — small, careful changes
lessons.objectives:
I can move my character only a little between frames
I can use onion skinning to line up the next frame
I can spot when something is not consistent
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: consistency · onion skinning · frame · careful. TA: tablets and the scene/characters from Lesson 3 ready; activity worksheet out. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Recap: last lesson we planned a simple story.
1. (0–10) Starter — what does ‘consistent’ mean? Teach: consistent means keeping things the SAME. On the starter worksheet pupils choose what to keep the same (multi-select) and match each problem to its fix.
   • Support: choose one thing to keep the same.  • Core: match problem to fix.  • Challenge: say what happens if the tablet moves.
2. (10–22) I-do → we-do — small changes. Teacher takes a frame, moves the character a tiny bit using onion skinning, takes the next. Likely error: moves the character a big jump → fix-words “only a little, then a photo.”
3. (22–42) You-do — film your animation. Pupils film their planned story with small, careful changes. On the activity worksheet they look at two frames and choose what changed a little (single-choice), then ORDER the careful-filming steps. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: spot the one thing that moved.  • Core: order the steps.  • Challenge: say why small changes look smoother.
4. (42–50) Plenary — watch a clip back; self-tick the 3 “I can…”. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
Note: the source ‘spot the change’ clips are teacher-played only.

lessons.title: Evaluate and make it great — review and improve
lessons.objectives:
I can watch my animation back and say what is good
I can choose one thing to make better
I can delete a frame I do not want
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: evaluate · improve · delete · frame · feedback. TA: tablets with last lesson’s animations; activity worksheet out. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Recap: last lesson we filmed our stories.
1. (0–10) Starter — what makes a good animation? Show the watch-back screen. Build a class checklist: smooth movement, tablet stayed still, no hands in shot. On the starter worksheet pupils tick what a good animation has (checklist).
   • Support: tick one thing.  • Core: tick all that are true.  • Challenge: add one more idea.
2. (10–22) I-do → we-do — watch back and tidy up. Teacher watches an animation back and deletes a frame with a hand in it. Likely error: keeps adding photos in the wrong place → fix-words “go to the end before you film more.”
3. (22–42) You-do — evaluate and improve. Pupils watch their animation, rate it on a 1–5 scale, choose one thing to improve, and make a better version. On the activity worksheet they answer what to do with an unwanted frame (single-choice) and rate their own animation (scale). TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: rate it on the scale.  • Core: choose one thing to improve.  • Challenge: say how you will improve it.
4. (42–50) Plenary — share one improvement; self-tick the 3 “I can…”. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
Note: the source watch-back clips are teacher-played only.

lessons.title: Lights, camera, action — adding music and titles
lessons.objectives:
I can name media I can add to a film, like music and a title
I can add media to my animation
I can say why my media makes the film better
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: media · import · title · music · transition. TA: tablets with the saved animations; the movie app open; activity worksheet out. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Recap: last lesson we made our animation the best it could be.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is media? Teach: media means extra things we add — music, a title, a picture, a sound. On the starter worksheet pupils SORT examples into media / not media.
   • Support: is music media (yes/no).  • Core: sort all the examples.  • Challenge: add one more piece of media.
2. (10–22) I-do → we-do — bring the animation into the movie app. Teacher imports the saved film, then adds a title and some music. Likely error: looks for media in the wrong tab → fix-words “music is in the Audio tab.”
3. (22–42) You-do — add your media. Pupils add a title and music to their film. On the activity worksheet they LABEL the movie app (Video, Photos, Audio), then choose why a title helps (single-choice). TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: find the Audio tab.  • Core: label the three parts.  • Challenge: say why your music fits the film.
4. (42–50) Plenary — watch a final film; rate it (scale); self-tick the 3 “I can…”. No flashing/sound; keep the volume low.
Note: any source example films are teacher-played only.
